coredata.dc:title: A trinity sensing platform for uric acid detection in clinical samples using Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot nanozymes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zihan
Chen, Yihong
Chen, Zexian
Duan, Jiarong
Wang, Huiping
Guo, Erhua
Zhi, Jianhui
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Li, Jun
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Designing and developing a simple and reliable method for detecting disease biomarkers is crucial for early disease prevention and diagnosis. Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot (Fe-CQD), synthesized from natural dandelion biomass through a controlled process, were developed as nanozymes with outstanding peroxidase-like activity. Uricase decomposes uric acid to release hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which the Fe-CQD nanozymes catalyze to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These ROS then oxidize the colorless substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), enabling visual detection of uric acid. Building on this principle, we have developed, for the first time, a visual sensing platform using Fe-CQD nanozymes. This platform integrates absorption spectra, a microplate reader, and smartphone-assisted visible-light imaging to detect uric acid, achieving detection limits as low as 0.31, 0.36, and 0.83 μM, respectively. The platform demonstrated excellent accuracy in clinical blood sample analysis, yielding results highly consistent with standard hospital methods and the UA Assay Kit-based colorimetric method. It also exhibited robust anti-interference capabilities and practicality, making it a reliable tool for UA detection in real-world clinical settings. This innovative platform highlights the potential of nanozymes in biomarker detection and lays a theoretical foundation for integrating intelligent, portable sensing technologies into clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Trinity sensing platform
Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot nanozymes
Peroxidase-like activity
Uric acid
Clinical samples

Preparation and characterization of the Fe-CQD
Steady-state kinetic analysis of the Fe-CQD
UA detection using the Fe-CQD nanozymes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (c) diameter distribution, and (e) EDXA spectra of CQD. (b) TEM image, (d) diameter distribution, and (f) EDXA spectra of Fe-CQD.
(a) XPS survey scan, (b) C 1s XPS, (c) N 1s XPS, and (f) FTIR spectra of CQD and Fe-CQD. (d) Cl 2p XPS and (e) Fe 2p XPS of Fe-CQD.
Effects of (a) Fe-CQD concentration, (b) temperature, (c) reaction time, and (d) pH on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQD.
Michaelis-Menten kinetic analysis of Fe-CQD for (a) OPD and (b) H2O2. Lineweaver-Burk plot of Fe-CQD for (c) OPD and (d) H2O2.

(a) Relative absorbance in the presence of ROS scavengers (IPA, BQ, and Try: 10 mM; Fe-CQD: 0.025 mg mL−1; OPD: 1.5 mM; H2O2: 0.25 mM; pH 1.0). (b) EPR spectra of Fe-CQD + H2O2 + TEMP in pH 1.0 (blue line), Fe-CQD + H2O2 + DMPO in pH 1.0 (green line), and Fe-CQD + H2O2 + DMPO in methanol (red line).

(a) Schematic illustration of the colorimetric mechanism of UA detection using Fe-CQD. (b) Selectivity of Fe-CQD toward different ions, anions, and amino acids. (c) Anti-interference capability of Fe-CQD across different analytes. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Fe-CQD at different UA concentrations. (e) Relationship between absorbance change at 452 nm (ΔA452) and UA concentration. (f) Images of UA solutions at different concentrations in a 96-well plate under visible light. (g) Correlation between ΔA452 from the microplate reader and UA concentration. (h) G-value analysis from 96-well plate images (left) and corresponding plot of G values versus UA concentrations (right).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Fe-CQD nanozymes and their application in UA detection.
Comparison of kinetic parameters of CQD-based nanozymes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CDs: carbon dots; Fe-CDs: Fe-doped carbon dots; Cu-CDs: copper-based carbon dots; Cu2O-CDs-Cu: Cuprous oxide-carbon dots-cuprous; CDs: carbon dots; Fe CDs/Mo SACs: iron-doped carbon dots/molybdenum single-atoms catalysts; FeMn/N-CDs: iron, cobalt, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots; Fe-CQD: Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Analytical comparison of UA levels in clinical serum samples using three different methods (n = 3). (b) Recovery analysis of UA in clinical serum samples (n = 3).
Comparison of Fe-CQD-based sensors with other reported sensors for UA detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: A-CDs: ascorbic acid-derived carbon dots; N,Fe-CDs/BSA-Ag NCs: N,Fe-doped carbon dots and bovine serum albumin-stabilized silver nanoclusters; CD@ZIF-CuNC: carbon dots and Cu nanoclusters; Fe@NCDs: carbon quantum dots co-doped with iron and nitrogen; FeCo/N-CDs: iron, cobalt and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots; N-CDs: nitrogen-doped carbon dots; Fe CDs/Mo SACs: iron-doped carbon dots adsorbed on molybdenum single-atoms catalysts; Fe-CQD: Fe-doped carbon quantum dot.

head.ce:title: A trinity sensing platform for uric acid detection in clinical samples using Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot nanozymes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nephrology II, Shanxi Traditional Chinese Medical Hospital, Taiyuan, 030012, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Urology, First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing and developing a simple and reliable method for detecting disease biomarkers is crucial for early disease prevention and diagnosis. Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot (Fe-CQD), synthesized from natural dandelion biomass through a controlled process, were developed as nanozymes with outstanding peroxidase-like activity. Uricase decomposes uric acid to release hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which the Fe-CQD nanozymes catalyze to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These ROS then oxidize the colorless substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), enabling visual detection of uric acid. Building on this principle, we have developed, for the first time, a visual sensing platform using Fe-CQD nanozymes. This platform integrates absorption spectra, a microplate reader, and smartphone-assisted visible-light imaging to detect uric acid, achieving detection limits as low as 0.31, 0.36, and 0.83 μM, respectively. The platform demonstrated excellent accuracy in clinical blood sample analysis, yielding results highly consistent with standard hospital methods and the UA Assay Kit-based colorimetric method. It also exhibited robust anti-interference capabilities and practicality, making it a reliable tool for UA detection in real-world clinical settings. This innovative platform highlights the potential of nanozymes in biomarker detection and lays a theoretical foundation for integrating intelligent, portable sensing technologies into clinical diagnostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hyperuricemia is a metabolic disorder marked by elevated serum uric acid (UA) levels in extracellular fluids and tissues, often accompanied by impaired uric acid excretion [1]. It is defined as a UA level of ≥7.0 mg dl−1 (416.0 μM) in males or ≥ 6.0 mg dl−1 (357.0 μM) in females [2]. This abnormal increase in UA can lead to a range of health issues, including renal disorder, gout, Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, and arthritis. In patients with gout, the accumulation of excess UA in the joints can cause acute inflammation and increase the risk of kidney stone formation [3]. Conversely, hypouricemia, which refers to low UA levels, is relatively rare and has been associated with conditions such as AIDS and diabetes mellitus [4]. Over the past few decades, several methods for detecting UA have been developed, including spectrophotometry [5], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], fluorescent probes [7], and gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [8]. However, these methods often require expensive equipment, complex preprocessing, and lengthy detection times. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a rapid, convenient, cost-effective, and accurate method for UA detection.

Recently, catalytic oxidation reactions using nanozymes have demonstrated great potential in chemical sensing, environmental monitoring, and biomedicine [9–11]. Nanozymes combine the unique enzymatic properties of biological enzymes with the advantages of nanomaterials, including easy synthesis, long shelf life, excellent reproducibility, high stability, adjustable catalytic activity, and cost-effectiveness [12,13]. Peroxidase-like nanozymes, for example, can catalyze reactions with chromogenic substrates such as o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2, producing fluorescent or colored products that amplify signals for highly sensitive detection [14]. Among the various nanozyme materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) stand out due to their small size, excellent stability, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity [15,16]. The simple preparation process, diverse precursor sources, and versatile structural properties of CQD nanozymes enhance their potential for developing high-performance sensing platforms [17]. For instance, doping with metal elements can further enhance the peroxidase-like activity of CQDs [18]. Recent studies have highlighted the innovative applications of nanozymes. Yang et al. [19] developed a novel photothermal system for detecting dichlorvos using coordinated carbon dots-Fe (CDs-Fe) with peroxidase-like activity. Lu et al. [20] created a molybdenum-doped carbon dots (Mo-CDs) nanozyme with excellent peroxidase-like activity for detecting ampicillin (AMP), integrating colorimetric test strips with smartphones for rapid and sensitive monitoring of AMP in water samples. Advancements in smartphone technology, particularly in high-definition cameras, have made them valuable tools for point-of-care testing (POCT) sensors [17,21–23]. Consequently, several colorimetric detection systems utilizing smartphones have been reported [24,25]. Developing an intelligent, integrated detection platform that combines CQD nanozyme reactions with smartphone technology is crucial for enabling accurate, sensitive, and convenient in-situ monitoring of UA. This highlights the significance of creating metal-functionalized CQD nanozymes for developing simple, efficient, and on-site sensors for rapid UA detection.

In this study, we synthesized iron (Fe)-functionalized carbon quantum dot (Fe-CQD) nanozymes using a simple hydrothermal synthesis process. These Fe-CQD nanozymes effectively catalyze H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2), exhibiting remarkable peroxidase-like activity. Leveraging this catalytic system, we developed a colorimetric sensor for UA detection. In the presence of UA, uricase catalyzes the breakdown of UA, producing H2O2. The Fe-CQD nanozymes then catalyze H2O2 to generate 1O2 and •OH, which oxidize OPD into form yellow DAP, resulting in a color change that facilitates detection. Additionally, we integrated this method into a sensing platform that combines the colorimetric analysis with smartphone technology. This platform enables rapid, accurate, and sensitive visual monitoring of UA in clinical blood samples (Scheme 1 ), providing results that align closely with those obtained from standard hospital testing. This approach not only expands the application of CQD nanozymes but also highlights the significant potential of Fe-CQD nanozymes in the detection and monitoring of UA.

Dandelions were bought from a local supermarket in Taiyuan, China. o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3•6H2O), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrochloric acid (HCl), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), 5,5ʹ-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), tryptophan (Try), alanine (Ala), valine (Val), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), leucine (Leu), homocysteine (Hcy), isoleucine (Ile), glutamine (Gln), glutathione (GSH), glycine (Gly), proline (Pro), cysteine (Cys), asparagine (Asp), serine (Ser), methionine (Met), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and other reagents were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). Methanol (MA) was obtained from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). UA Assay Kit was bought from Nanjing Jiancheng Technology Company (Nanjing, China). All chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ•cm), obtained from a Molecular Ultrapure Water System (Shanghai, China), was used throughout the experiments.

Fe-CQD was synthesized by dissolving 0.5 g of dandelion powder and 0.3 g of FeCl3•6H2O in 10 mL of ultrapure water, followed by hydrothermal treatment in an autoclave at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting solution was filtered, and the solid precipitate was purified using a 0.22 μm filtration membrane and dialyzed for 8 h with a 100 Da dialysis membrane. The final Fe-CQD nanozyme powder was obtained through freeze-drying. As a control, CQD was synthesized under the same conditions without FeCl3•6H2O.

CQD and Fe-CQD were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Detailed instrument parameters are available in our previous publications [21,22].

The peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQD nanozymes was assessed by monitoring the catalytic oxidation of OPD to DAP in the presence of H2O2. Specifically, Fe-CQD (0.025 mg mL−1), H2O2 (0.25 mM), and OPD (1.5 mM) were mixed in 2.0 mL of HCl solution (pH = 1.0) and incubated at 30 °C for 20 min. UV–Vis absorption at 452 nm was recorded to track the reaction progress. To optimize the peroxidase-like activity, the effects of OPD concentration (0.0–1.21 mM), H2O2 concentration (0.16–3.30 mM), Fe-CQD concentration (4.90–36.73 μg mL−1), pH (1.0–8.0), reaction temperature (10–40 °C), and reaction time (1–15 min) were systematically evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic analysis of Fe-CQD nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic kinetics of Fe-CQD nanozymes were investigated using OPD and H2O2 as substrates. Solutions containing Fe-CQD (0.025 mg mL−1) with 1.5 mM OPD (or 0.25 mM H2O2) were prepared, and varying concentrations of H2O2 (or OPD) were introduced. Absorbance at 452 nm was measured, and reaction rates were analyzed using the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot:
1 V
=
K m
V max
[ S ]
+
1
V max
where V, Km, Vmax, and [S] represent the initial reaction velocity, the Michaelis-Menten constant, the maximum reaction velocity, and the substrate concentration, respectively.

To elucidate the mechanism underlying the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQD, various reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavengers, including IPA (10 mM), Try (10 mM), and BQ (10 mM), were introduced into the OPD oxidation system. The reaction mixture contained HCl (pH = 1.0), Fe-CQD (0.025 mg mL−1), and OPD (1.5 mM), and absorption spectra were recorded. Additionally, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was employed to detect ROS intermediates. TEMP (10 mM in ethanol) was used to capture 1O2, while DMPO was applied for •OH and superoxide anions (•O2 −). TEMP was added to a mixture of Fe-CQD (0.025 mg mL−1) and H2O2 (0.25 mM), and EPR analysis was conducted after a 5-min reaction. For DMPO-based detection, 10 mM DMPO was dissolved in either HCl solution (pH = 1.0) or methanol to trap •OH and •O2 −, respectively.

A 5.0 mg mL−1 Fe-CQD nanozyme solution was prepared in ultrapure water. Stock solutions (0.01 M) of various amino acids (Ala, Val, Lys, Arg, Leu, Hcy, Ile, Gln, GSH, Try, Gly, Pro, Cys, Asp, Ser, and Met), metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, and Cl−), AA, urea, and UA were prepared for further analysis. To evaluate Fe-CQD selectivity for UA, each substance (32.1 mM) was mixed with uricase (0.05 mg mL−1) at 30 °C for 30 min, followed by the addition of OPD (1.5 mM) and Fe-CQD (0.025 mg mL−1) and incubation for 20 min. For interference studies, each potential interfering substance (0.16 mM) was combined with UA and uricase under identical conditions. UV–Vis absorbance was then recorded. To determine the limit of detection (LOD) and linear range, a UA titration series was prepared, and absorbance was measured using a microplate reader. A white luminous plate was integrated into the visible light source to ensure stable lighting for image capture. Smartphone images of the reaction solutions were taken at fixed angles and positions to maintain consistency and minimize background interference. Image analysis was performed using Color Identification software on the smartphone.

Serum samples were collected with approval from the Committee of Medical Ethics at Shanxi University (Approval No. SXULL2024123), following all relevant ethical regulations. Samples from healthy volunteers and individuals with elevated UA levels were stored at −20 °C and diluted with water (10 %) before spectroscopic analysis to minimize background interference. In the presence of uricase, UA is enzymatically converted to allantoin and H2O2, which subsequently reacts with OPD, forming yellow DPA. The reaction was monitored by measuring absorbance at 452 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the Fe-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot (Fe-CQD) was synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method, with dandelion powder and FeCl3•6H2O as the reaction precursors. For comparison, carbon quantum dot (CQD) was fabricated without FeCl3•6H2O. Extensive optimization studies were conducted to determine the ideal precursor ration, reaction time, and reaction temperature (Fig. S1). The results indicated that the optimal mass ratio of FeCl3•6H2O to dandelion powder was 3:5 (Fig. S1a), with the highest peroxidase-like activity achieved at a reaction time of 6 h (Fig. S1b) and a reaction temperature of 200 °C (Fig. S1c). The Fe-CQD prepared under these conditions were used in all subsequent experiments.

The morphology and size of both CQD and Fe-CQD were characterized using TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 1 a and b shows that both particle types are uniformly dispersed, with lattice spacings of 0.23 nm for CQD and 0.27 nm for Fe-CQD on the copper grid. The average particle sizes, depicted in Fig. 1c and d, were 2.38 ± 0.2 nm for CQD and 1.69 ± 0.1 nm for Fe-CQD. The particle size distributions ranged from 1.62 nm to 3.14 nm for CQD and from 1.23 nm to 2.25 nm for Fe-CQD, demonstrating that Fe doping has a minimal effect on the size of the CQD. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDXA) revealed that CQD is primarily composed of C, N, and O (Fig. 1e), while Fe-CQD contains C, N, O, Cl, and Fe (Fig. 1f). The EDXA spectrum of Fe-CQD displays that the distribution of Fe is closely aligned with that of N, suggesting a uniform distribution of Fe within the Fe-CQD. This observation implies a potential interaction between Fe and N, which may help stabilize Fe and supports its nanozyme activity.

To investigate the surface functional groups and chemical composition of CQD and Fe-CQD, XPS and FTIR were conducted. The XPS survey scan of CQD (Fig. 2 a) reveals three characteristic peaks at 529.0, 397.0, and 282.0 eV, corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively. Quantitative analysis using integral area and rate sensitivity factors (RSF) gives the following atomic percentages: 53.3 % C, 18.4 % N, and 28.3 % O. For Fe-CQD, the XPS survey scan (Fig. 2a) displays five peaks at 712.0, 529.0, 397.0, 282.0, and 196.0 eV, corresponding to Fe 2p, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and Cl 2p, respectively. The integral area and RSF analysis indicate the atomic contents as 52.9 % C, 23.1 % N, 20.8 % O, 1.4 % Fe, and 1.8 % Cl. The C 1s XPS spectra for both CQD and Fe-CQD (Fig. 2b) exhibit five peaks at 284.3, 284.8, 285.4, 286.3, and 288.2 eV, attributed to C–C, CC, C–N, C–O, and O–CO, respectively [26–28]. In the N 1s XPS spectrum of CQD (Fig. 2c), peaks at 399.4, 400.1, and 401.0 eV correspond to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [22,29]. In contrast, the Fe-CQD retains pyridinic N (399.4 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.1 eV), while graphitic N is absent. Additionally, a new Fe–N peak at 402.1 eV is observed (Fig. 2c), confirming the coordination of Fe with N in Fe-CQD [30]. The O 1s XPS spectra of both CQD and Fe-CQD (Fig. S2) show five peaks at 530.8, 531.4, 531.9, 532.6, and 533.4 eV, corresponding to O–N, OC, C–OH, O–C, and O–Cl, respectively [19,21,31]. The Cl 2p XPS spectrum of Fe-CQD (Fig. 2d) depicts three peaks at 197.7, 198.4, and 199.7 eV, corresponding to Cl–O, Cl–N, and Cl–C, respectively [32]. The Fe 2p XPS spectrum of Fe-CQD (Fig. 2e) displays four peaks at 711.0, 714.3, 724.0 and 726.7 eV, attributing to Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe3+ 2p3/2, Fe2+ 2p1/2, and Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively [19,30,33]. Moreover, FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2f) was used to identify functional groups in both CQD and Fe-CQD and confirm their chemical composition. Characteristic peaks corresponding to C–O, CC, CO, C–H, and O–H/N–H stretching were observed at 1090, 1410, 1591, 2951, and 3390 cm−1, respectively [34,35]. The FTIR spectrum of Fe-CQD also reveals a peak at 599 cm−1, corresponding to Fe–N stretching vibrations [19,30]. Overall, the XPS and FTIR results confirm the successful doping of Fe into Fe-CQD, with Fe primarily present in the form of Fe–N coordination, which is integral to the enhanced catalytic activity of the nanozymes.

Low cytotoxicity is a crucial property for the biological application of CQD. We thoroughly investigated the biocompatibility of Fe-CQD. The cytotoxicity of Fe-CQD was assessed by evaluating the viability of LO2 cells using the CCK-8 assay. LO2 cells were incubated with various concentrations of Fe-CQD (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, and 300 μg mL−1) for 24 h. As shown in Fig. S3a, even at the highest concentration (300 mg mL−1), cell viability remained above 90 %, indicating that the Fe-CQD exhibits low cytotoxicity. To evaluate bacterial toxicity, E. coli was used as a model organism. As displayed in Figs. S3b and S3c, E coli was incubated with Fe-CQD at concentrations from 0 to 300 mg mL−1 for 10 h. At the highest concentration (300 mg mL−1), no significant change in the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was observed, and colonies grew normally on solid medium. These findings suggest that the Fe-CQD does not exhibit significant bacterial toxicity. In conclusion, the successful doping of N, Fe, and Cl into Fe-CQD contributes to their excellent biocompatibility, highlighting their promising potential for various biological applications.

The presence of Fe in the Fe-CQD was confirmed through TEM and XPS analyses. The redox reactions involving Fe in different valence states revealed promising potential for biocatalysis applications. Consequently, we investigated the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe-CQD, utilizing the typical OPD oxidation reaction in the presence of H2O2 as a model system. In this reaction, peroxidase enzymes catalyze the oxidation of OPD by H2O2, producing DAP, which results in a color change from colorless to yellow, along with an absorption peak at 452 nm [19,29]. As depicted in Fig. S4a, no significant absorption changes were observed in the Fe-CQD + OPD group and other control groups. However, a prominent absorption peak at 452 nm and a noticeable yellow color change were observed in the group treated with H2O2, Fe-CQD, and OPD. This confirmed the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQD in catalyzing the oxidation of OPD. To further verify that Fe doping is essential for this activity, we synthesized unfunctionalized CQD using only dandelion powder as the precursor. This unfunctionalized CQD did not catalyze the oxidation of OPD, clearly demonstrating that Fe doping is crucial for the peroxidase-like activity (Fig. S4b). Several factors can influence the peroxidase-like activity of nanozymes, including nanozyme concentration, pH, temperature, and reaction time [20]. We examined the effects of varying concentrations of Fe-CQD (4.90–36.73 μg mL−1), pH (1.0–8.0), reaction temperature (10–40 °C), and reaction time (1–15 min) on the enzyme-like activity. As depicted in Fig. 3 a–c, increasing concentrations of Fe-CQD, higher temperatures, and longer reaction times enhanced peroxidase-like activity. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of Fe-CQD was found to be pH-dependent. Fig. 3d indicates that the highest catalytic activity occurred at pH 1.0. These results confirm that the Fe-CQD possess strong peroxidase-like activity, providing a foundation for further studies on their catalytic behavior and applications in various sensing platforms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic analysis of the Fe-CQD
ce:section.ce:para:
Steady-state kinetic analysis was performed under optimal conditions using OPD and H2O2 as substrates to evaluate the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe-CQD. As displayed in Fig. 4 a and b, the catalytic reactions of both substrates followed the standard Michaelis-Menten model. The Michaelis constant (K
m ) and maximum initial velocity (V
max ) for OPD and H2O2 were determined from the intercept and slope of the Burk double reciprocal plots (Fig. 4c and d). The K
m values for OPD and H2O2 were determined to be 0.022 μM and 1.94 μM, respectively. These values suggest that Fe-CQD exhibit a superior affinity for OPD compared to other peroxidase-like nanozymes [29,36–41]. The Vmax values for OPD and H2O2 were 2.74 × 10−8 M s−1 and 13.33 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively, which are both higher than those of most other reported nanozymes. These findings underscore the Fe-CQD's exceptional catalytic activity and confirm its potential as a novel type of nanozyme for future biocatalytic applications. The results are summarized in Table 1 , which compares the kinetic parameters of Fe-CQD with those of other peroxidase-like nanozymes.

To elucidate the catalytic mechanism of the Fe-CQD nanozymes, we analyzed the types of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during the catalytic reaction. Try, 1,4-BQ, and IPA were used as scavengers for 1O2, •O2 −, and •OH, respectively [20]. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe-CQD significantly decreased when Try and IPA were added. However, the addition of BQ had little effect on the peroxidase activity, suggesting that 1O2 and •OH play key roles in the catalytic process, while •O2 − does not contribute significantly to the reaction. Further confirmation of 1O2, •O2 −, and •OH generation was obtained using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. To verify 1O2 production, an EPR experiment was conducted using TEMP as the spin trap for 1O2. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the Fe-CQD + H2O2 + TEMP system produced three characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1, which is consistent with the formation of 1O2. Similarly, to confirm the generation of •OH, we used DMPO as the spin trap. In the DMPO + Fe-CQD + H2O2 system at pH 1.0, a typical EPR signal with a peak intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 was observed, confirming the formation of the DMPO-OH spin adduct. These results indicate that 1O2 and •OH are the primary ROS involved in the Fe-CQD nanozyme-catalyzed oxidation of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UA detection using the Fe-CQD nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Under acidic conditions, colorless OPD undergoes oxidation to form yellow DPA, a reaction commonly employed in colorimetric detection. This process is typically slow without H2O2. However, Fe-CQD, enhanced by Fe doping, efficiently decomposes H2O2 to generate 1O2 and •OH, accelerating OPD oxidation and producing a rapid color change. In this detection strategy, uricase catalyzes the breakdown of UA, generating H2O2, which is subsequently catalyzed by Fe-CQD to produce 1O2 and •OH. These reactive species further oxidize OPD to DAP, yielding a distinct absorption peak at 452 nm, enabling UA detection (Fig. 6 a). To evaluate the Fe-CQD catalytic system's effectiveness, we examined its selectivity and sensitivity toward UA. As shown in Fig. 6b, the system exhibited strong specificity, effectively distinguishing UA from various ions, amino acids, and other interfering substances. Additionally, the system demonstrated excellent anti-interference capability, as the presence of multiple ions and compounds had no significant impact on absorbance (Fig. 6c). This high selectivity and resistance to interference confirm the reliability of Fe-CQD for UA detection, even in complex sample matrices.

To enhance detection stability and reproducibility, we optimized key parameters, including uricase concentration, incubation time and temperature, Fe-CQD concentration, and reaction duration with OPD. The optimal conditions were determined as follows: uricase concentration of 47.85 μg mL−1 (Fig. S5a), incubation time of 30 min (Fig. S5b), temperature of 30 °C (Fig. S5c), Fe-CQD concentration of 46.95 μg mL−1 (Fig. S5d), and reaction time of 20 min (Fig. S5e). Under these conditions, we established a reliable colorimetric platform for UA detection. To assess sensitivity and dynamic range, we introduced varying UA concentrations into the reaction system. Under optimized conditions, the absorbance at 452 nm (A452) increased proportionally with UA concentration (Fig. 6d). A strong linear correlation was observed between A452 and UA concentration in the range of 0.23–93.02 μM (ΔA452 = 0.0043[UA] + 0.0705, R2 = 0.9920), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.31 μM (Fig. 6e).

To enhance practicality and enable portable, on-site UA detection, we integrated a microplate reader and a smartphone-assisted imaging system for UA quantification. Initially, uricase and UA were incubated at 30 °C for 30 min, followed by the addition of Fe-CQD and OPD, with a further 20 min incubation at 30 °C in a 96-well plate. As displayed in Fig. 6f, increasing UA concentrations produced distinct color changes. Using a microplate reader, we recorded absorbance at 452 nm (A452), which exhibited a linear correlation with UA concentration in the range of 2.23–93.02 μM (ΔA452 = 0.0038[UA] + 0.1313, R2 = 0.9926), with an LOD of 0.36 μM (Fig. 6g). For enhanced portability, we coupled the 96-well plate system with a smartphone-based color recognition method for visual analysis. A smartphone camera captured images of the plate, and color-recognition software converted the images into RGB values (Fig. 6h). A calibration curve was generated by plotting G values against UA concentration. As depicted in Fig. 6h, the G values correlated linearly with UA concentrations from 2.33 to 186.05 μM, yielding a regression equation of G = −0.132[UA] + 180.31 (R2 = 0.9927) with an LOD of 0.83 μM. This smartphone-assisted detection platform demonstrated high sensitivity and reliability, offering a convenient and effective approach for UA detection in real-world applications. Overall, the Fe-CQD-based colorimetric system exhibited strong selectivity and sensitivity, making it a promising tool for portable and on-site analysis.

In summary, Fe-functionalized carbon quantum dot (Fe-CQD) were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of dandelion powder and FeCl3·6H2O, exhibiting excellent biocompatibility and peroxidase-like activity. Leveraging these properties, we developed a trinity sensing platform for UA detection, integrating colorimetric, microplate reader, and smartphone-assisted imaging methods. The platform demonstrated high sensitivity, with detection limits of 0.31 μM (colorimetry), 0.36 μM (microplate reader), and 0.83 μM (smartphone-assisted imaging). It also offered broad detection ranges: 0.23–93.02 μM for colorimetry, 2.33–93.02 μM for the microplate reader, and 2.33–186.05 μM for smartphone-assisted imaging. Additionally, it exhibited strong anti-interference performance, high recovery rates (94.2 %–103.8 %), and outstanding reproducibility (RSDs ≤ 8.1 %), confirming its reliability for clinical sample analysis. This rapid, sensitive, and portable detection method provides a promising tool for serum UA monitoring and broader disease biomarker detection applications.

Zihan Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yihong Chen: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zexian Chen: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiarong Duan: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Huiping Wang: Formal analysis. Erhua Guo: Software. Jianhui Zhi: Formal analysis. Shaomin Shuang: Writing – review & editing. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Project administration. Jun Li: Project administration, Investigation. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705101 and 22274090), Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development (YDZJSX2021A004 and YDZJSX2022C002), and Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202403021212049 and 202103021224016).
